# Almuñécar: three millennia of living history

Almuñécar is home to a registered population of 27,600 inhabitants, with an economy that combines a tourism industry offering 25,000 beds with an agricultural production of 15,000 tons of cherimoya annually, holding a Protected Designation of Origin. This dual model attracts both seasonal European tourists and permanent residents from 60 different nationalities. Managing this ecosystem, from the urban core to the 97 surrounding hamlets and farmsteads, ensures a balance between Phoenician heritage and modern agricultural sustainability.

## The weight of stone and salt

The history of Almuñécar is not read in books; it is felt underfoot in its streets. From the founding of Sexi by the Phoenicians in the 8th century B.C. to the walls of the Castle of San Miguel, every layer tells a different ambition. It is not a static museum; it is a sedimentation. Here, the past has not disappeared; it has been integrated into the daily routines of those who buy bread under arches that have seen Romans and Nasrids pass by—a constant reminder that civilization is an exercise in persistence against the erosion of the sea.

## The architecture of water in El Majuelo

The El Majuelo Botanical Park preserves 180 species of tropical and subtropical plants, alongside the most important fish-salting pits in the Western Mediterranean. This enclave is not merely a garden; it is a lesson in ancient economy. The Phoenicians did not arrive here by chance, but for the salt and the tuna, creating an industry that anchored regional trade for centuries. Today, to look at those stone vats is to understand that the wealth of this coast has always depended on knowing how to read the generosity of the current.

## The cherimoya as an identity of the soil

The cherimoya of the Costa Tropical, cultivated on the hillsides of Almuñécar and its valleys, reaches an annual production exceeding 12,000 tons under the seal of its Designation of Origin. This fruit, delicate and temperamental, has shaped the rural landscape, turning arid lands into a productive orchard. It is not just agriculture; it is a cultural landscape where the local farmer is the primary guardian of the terrain. Protecting this crop is not just a matter of the market; it is maintaining the dignity of a labor that defies gravity on the slopes.

## Coexistence in modernity

The social fabric of Almuñécar has been transformed into a mosaic where the descendants of local fishermen live alongside an international community that chooses this municipality for its climate stability. With more than 6,000 foreign residents, the challenge is integration without the loss of local memory. The success of this blend lies in the fact that no one tries to change the identity of the other; the foreigner learns to respect the rhythm of the siesta and the schedule of the processions, while the city opens itself to globalization without selling its soul to cheap concrete.

## The future between two waters

The balance between tourism and the preservation of heritage is the tightrope upon which the future of this municipality walks. The commitment to rehabilitating spaces like the Palacete de La Najarra or the routes toward the cliffs of Maro is the correct roadmap. It is not about attracting more people, but about offering a story worth hearing. The Costa Tropical must continue to be that place where the traveler, upon arrival, feels they have found something that, at last, has weight and roots, far from the artifice of transient tourism.

The true Almuñécar is not to be found in brightly colored guidebooks, but in the silence of a June afternoon, when the Mediterranean breeze travels up the Río Verde and cools the stones of the castle. It is a place for men and women who still know how to value the pause, the ripe fruit, and a history that can be told without the need to raise one's voice.

Until next time — the Coast awaits you, from Almuñécar to the Axarquía.
